entry.title: How to use hexo to create page, tag and category
entry.content:
Once setting up the hexo environment, it’s time to create your new pages! Shall we just use mkdir cmd to create subfolders under source folder? The answer is NO. Please realize this is HEXO! We could use “hexo cmd” directly!

1. create new file

  • cmd
    hexo new <title>

the file would be generated under hexo/source/_posts folder

  • settings

2. create new tag

  • cmd
    hexo new page tags

  • settings

3. create new category

  • cmd
    hexo new page categories

  • settings

for more information, please go to hexo website

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: VIM - common used cmd!
entry.content:
case 1 - to delete and add same content in multi lines

before change

add

  1. move the cursor at the first line first character
  2. ctrl + v and enter visual block mode
  3. use motion key or run “3gg” to select all
  4. input I (upper case) to enter Edit mode
  5. input the content you want to insert
  6. ESC and wait for a moment

after change

delete

  1. move the cursor at the first line first character
  2. ctrl + v and enter visual block mode
  3. use motion key or run “3j3l” to select what you want to delete
  4. enter “d” to delete
    tip: after delete, we could still use ‘p’ to paste the deleted sentence which means ‘delete’ is like ‘cut’.

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: Cannot connect Raspberry Pi after a period of time?
entry.content:
These days, humidifier in the study is working day and night since I wanna reduce formaldehyde content(that’s another story). Around one week later, ghosting appeared on my display screen and finally the root cause turned out to be the humidifier’s water vapor. Moving the monitor to the balcony and under the sunshine, then it works well again.

What drives me crazy is that my Raspberry Pi refused to work almost at the same time! I used to connect my Raspberry Pi via SSH while this time it showed “connection refused”. I was afraid that it was due to the vapor as well and disappointed when it still had no response after one day’s in the sun.

This Saturday, getting time to dig further, I connected the mouse, keyboard and monitor to my lovely Pi, and it’s working! How came I could not SSH to it? It’s the IP address changing!

By default, the Raspberry Pi will get a dynamically allocated IP address, meaning it changes as you restart it or potentially when new devices are added to the network. We could set a static IP address to solve this issue.

[reference] https://www.ionos.com/digitalguide/server/configuration/provide-raspberry-pi-with-a-static-ip-address/

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: Stringize(#) and token pasting (##) operator in C language
entry.content:
Stringizing operator

convert a token to string

Token passing operator

Token pasting operator (##) combines or concatenates tokens together.

example:

###Reference:
[1] https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/preprocessor/stringizing-operator-hash?view=msvc-170
[2] https://codeforwin.org/2018/11/stringize-token-pasting-operator-in-c-language.html

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: To generate random numbers in C
entry.content:
For randomly gerenating number, we could use <stdlib.h> header file in order to leverage the rand function. Each time rand() is called, it would generate a totally random number. However, it would generate the same value if the environment/program kept unchanging. Thus here we introduce another function srand() which is used to create a seed value. The input parameter could set as time since time changes and makes sure the seed various.

To generate floating number, we could type cast value to (float)rand();

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

categories.category: Real-Time Embedded Systems Concepts and Practices

entry.title: RTES - 2 - Assignment 1
entry.content:
Requirement

  1. Print the output of uname -a in the first line of the syslog file.
  2. Each syslog statement should have [COURSE:X][ASSIGNMENT:Y] where X corresponds to Course Number i.e. 1 in this case and Y corresponds to Assignment No.

Example - Aug 30 22:10:54 raspberrypi pthread:
[COURSE:1][ASSIGNMENT:1] YOUR DATA HERE

This is what your output should look like. Also, please note that the uname output is generated from the program and not copied from the terminal and pasted into the program as a string.
All syslog prints in this assignment and upcoming assignments shall follow this format. (The time, name of the machine, and the program name are generated by syslog, you do not need to add them in manually).
3. This assignment expects two log statements as below from the main and the thread in execution

Hello World from Main!
Hello World from Thread!

Solution

1. how to log uname -a info into syslog?

a. use system function

  • using man system to get how to use this interface. please note that we need to #include <stdlib.h>
  • system("logger [COURSE:1][ASSIGNMENT:1]: `uname -a`“);`

b. print uname struct elements (this makes things a little bit complicated)

  • uname struct

reference: https://www.man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/uname.2.html

  • corresponding code

it has disadvantages since there is no operation system info in the struct.

2. how to use syslog

  • openlog() and closelog()

3. the whole code

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

categories.category: Real-Time Embedded Systems Concepts and Practices

entry.title: how to open a file automatically after 'hexo new'?
entry.content:
By default, when we use hexo new to create files, the files will be located in _posts folder.

Each time after creating, we need to copy the path above to open the file using vim or any other application you like. Below is the procedure to open the file automatically using JavaScript. It’s convenient and simple, please try it~

Steps:

step 1:

Create a folder named scripts under hexo using **mkdir scripts**

step 2:

Change the _config.yml to avoid rendering files under scripts.

step 3:

Create a .js file under scripts folder.

Please note if you are using Windows, the command open -a shall be changed to start, you could directly run the cmd in your cmd prompt to make sure it works.

!!! Please note there is a space right after .app".

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: how to write your motivation letter?
entry.content:
1. For different job, do enough research and write different motivation letter. Some people like to write only one general letter and use it for all applications which is awful.

  2. Be sincere, write down your own story. Do not be boastful or unrealistic. Do not care much about using complicated sentences.

  3. Usually one page is enough. Professors or committee normally have no time to scan a long letter. Make sure your first paragraph is impressive.

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: 6.087-1-Introduction. Writing, compiling, and debugging C programs. Hello world.
entry.content:
1. What is C?

1.1 widely used today

a. extends to newer system architectures
b. efficiency/performance
c. low-level access --> often means faster

1.2 what is C used for?

a. OSes, like Linux
b. microcontrollers: automobiles and airplanes
c. embedded processors: phones, portable electronics, etc
d. DSP processors: digital audio and TV systems

1.3 C lacks:

a. exceptions
b. range-checking
c. garbage collection
d. object-oriented programming
e. polymorphism

2. Writing C Programs

2.1 steps

  1. edit .c file directly
  2. gcc compiler to get .o file
    gcc -Wall infilename.c -o outfilename.o
    // embed debugging info and disable optimization
    gcc -g -o0 -Wall infilename.c -o outfilename.o

3. Our First C Program

3.1 structure of a .c file

  1. begin with comments about file contents
  2. insert #include statements and preprocessor definitions
  3. function prototypes and variable declarations
  4. main() function
  5. define other functions

3.2 conditional preprocessor macros

#if, #ifdef, #ifndef, #else, #elif, #endif

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: 6.087-1-assignment
entry.content:
Problem Set 1

Problem 1.1

(a) what do curly braces denote in C? Why does it make sense to use curly braces to surround the body of a function?

anw：

  • Curly braces mean a block of code with the aim to complete one function
  • a function is a set of code to achieve one goal, curly brace makes it a whole block

(b) Describe the difference between the literal values 7, “7”, and ‘7’
anw：

  • 7 is a number, it could be int. “7” is a string, ‘7’ is a char.

(c) Consider the statement

Rewrite this statement, inserting parentheses to ensure that ans = 11.0 upon evaluation of this statement.

ans：

Problem 1.2

Consider the statement

For each of the four versions of the function macro squared() below, write the corresponding value of ans.

  1. #define squared(x) x*x

ans: ans = 18.0/2+1*2+1 = 9.0+3 = 12.0

  1. #define squared(x) (x*x)

ans: ans = 18.0/(2+1*2+1) = 18.0/5 = 3.6

  1. #define squared(x) (x)*(x)

ans: ans = 18.0/(2+1)(2+1) = 6.03 = 18.0

  1. #define squared(x) ((x)*(x))

ans: ans = 18.0/((2+1)*(2+1)) = 18.0/9 = 2.0

Problem 1.3

Write the “Hello, 6.087 students” program described in lecture in your favorite text editor and compile and execute it. Turn in a printout or screen shot showing

  • the command used to compile your program
  • the command used to execute your program (using gdb)
  • the output of your program

ans:

Problem 1.4

see 1_4.c

Problem 1.5

For each of the following statements, explain why it is not correct, and fix it.

(a) #include <stdio.h>;

ans: no need semicolon

include <stdio.h>

(b)

ans: the return type should be the same as input type

(c)

ans:

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: 6.087-2-Variables and datatypes, operators
entry.content:
1. Variables and data types

1.1 Definition

Datatype: The datatype of an object in memory determines the set of values it can have and what operations that can be performed on it. C is a weakly typed language. It allows implicit conversions as well as forced casting.

Variable: A variable is as named link/reference to a value stored in the system’s memory or an expression that can be evaluated.

1.2 Big endian vs. little endian

  • why we have these two formats?
    For numeric data types that span multiple bytes, the order of arrangement of the individual bytes is important.
  • definition
    Big endian: the most significant bits (MSBs) occupy the lower address.
    Little endian: the least significant bits (LSBs) occupy the lower address. (using this to remember, LLL)

1.3 Relational operators

Relational operators compare two operands to produce a ‘boolean’ result. In C, any non-zero value is considered to be ‘true’ and 0 is considered to be false.

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: 6.087-2-assignment
entry.content:
Problem 2.1

Determine the size,minimum and maximum value following data types. Please specify if your
machine is 32 bit or 64 bits in the answer.
• char
• unsigned char
• short
• int
• unsigned int
• unsigned long
• float
Hint: Use sizeof() operator,limits.h and float.h header files

ans:

Problem 2.2

Write logical expressions that tests whether a given character variable c is
• lower case letter
• upper case letter
• digit
• white space (includes space,tab,new line)

ans:

Problem 2.3

Consider int val=0xCAFE; Write expressions using bitwise operators that do the following:
• test if atleast three of last four bits (LSB) are on
• reverse the byte order (i.e., produce val=0xFECA)
• rotate fourbits (i.e., produce val=0xECAF)
ans:

  1. (val&0XF)|(val&0X7)|(val&0XB)|(val&0XD)|(val&0XE)
  2. (val&0XFF<<8)|(val&0XFF00>>8)
  3. (val&0XF<<12)|(val>>4)

Problem 2.4

Using precedence rules, evaluate the following expressions and determine the value of the variables(without running the code). Also rewrite them using parenthesis to make the order explicit.
• Assume (x=0xFF33,MASK=0xFF00).Expression: c=x & MASK ==0;
• Assume (x=10,y=2,z=2;).Expression: z=y=x++ + ++y∗2;
• Assume (x=10,y=4,z=1;).Expression: y>>= x&0x2 && z

ans:

  1. c = 0, c = x & (MASK==0);
  2. x=11, z=16, y=16, z = y = (x++) + ++(y*2)
  3. y >>= (x&0x2) && z; y = y>>1; x=10, y=2, z=1

comment:
the answer to the second one should be z=y=(x++)+ ((++y)*2), therefore x=11, y=3, z=10+6=16

Problem 2.5

Determine if the following statements have any errors. If so, highlight them and explain why.
• int 2nd_value=10;
• Assume (x=0,y=0,alliszero=1). alliszero =(x=1) && (y=0);
• Assume (x=10,y=3,z=0;). y=++x+y;z=z−−>x;
• Assume that we want to test if last four bits of x are on. (int MASK=0xF;ison=x&MASK==MASK)

  1. number can not be the first character.
  2. alliszero = (x==1) && (y==0)
  3. correct
  4. ison = (x&MASK)==MASK

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: 6.087-3-Control flow. Functions and modular programming. Variable scope. Static and global variables.
entry.content:
1. Blocks and Compound Statements

  • block can substitute for simple statement
  • compiled as a single unit

2. Control Flow

  • Unlike C++ or Java, C89/C90 has no boolean type util C99.
  • Expression must be numeric (or a pointer)

2.1 Conditional Statement

  • if or switch statement
  • for switch, integer (or character) variable as input

2.2 Loops

  • while for do-while break continue
  • for for loop, expressions can be empty, condition assumed to be “true”
  • break: exits innermost loop or switch statement to exit early

3. Functions

  • variables declared outside of a function block are globals
  • minimize transfer of state between functions

4. Modular Programming

  • extern keyword
    It’s a good habit to declare the global variable in .c file and then use extern in .h file.

5. Variable Scope

5.1 Static Variables

  • static keyword has two meanings, depending on where the static variable is declared
  1. Outside a function, `static` variables/functions only visible within that file, not globally (cannot be `extern`ed)

  2. Inside a function, `static` variables:
  • are still local to that function
  • are initialized only during program initialization
  • do not get reinitialized with each function call

5.2 Register Variables

  • during execution, data processed in registers
  • registers do not reside in addressed memory; pointer of a register variable illegal

Copyright © 2022- Emily Peng All Rights Reserved

entry.title: 6.087-3-assignment
entry.content:
Problem 3.1

Code profiling and registers. In this problem, we will use some basic code profiling to examine the effects of explicitly declaring variables as registers. Consider the fibonacci sequence generating function fibonacci in prob1.c, which is reproduced at the end of this problem set (and can be downloaded from Stellar). The main() function handles the code profiling, calling fibonacci() many times and measuring the average processor time.

(a) First, to get a baseline (without any explicitly declared registers), compile and run prob1.c. Code profiling is one of the rare cases where using a debugger like gdb is discouraged, because the debugger’s overhead can impact the execution time. Also, we want to turn off compiler optimization. Please use the following commands to compile and run the program:

How long does a single iteration take to execute (on average)?

ans:

(b) Now, modify the fibonacci() function by making the variables a, b, and c register variables. Recompile and run the code. How long does a single iteration take now, on average? Turn in a printout of your modified code (the fibonacci() function itself would suffice).

ans:

(c) Modify the fibonacci() function one more time by making the variable n also a register variable. Recompile and run the code once more. How long does a single iteration take with all four variables as register variables?
ans:

(d) Comment on your observed results. What can you conclude about using registers in your code?
ans:
Using registers in code would make it quicker. And the result would be more obvious if the variable is frequently called.

Problem 3.2

We are writing a simple searchable dictionary using modular programming. First, the program reads a file containing words and their definitions into an easily searchable data structure. Then, the user can type a word, and the program will search the dictionary, and assuming the word is found, outputs the definition. The program proceeds until the user chooses to quit. We split the code into several files: main.c, dict.c, and dict.h. The contents of these files are described briefly below.

Answer the following questions based on the above program structure.
(a) In implementing this program, you want to access the global variable the dictionary from main.c, as well as from dict.c. However, due to the header file’s inclusion in both source documents, the variable gets declared in both places, creating an ambiguity. How would you resolve this ambiguity?
ans:
This is a good question!
I will declare the variable in dict.c, then extern it in dict.h. Then main.c could include the dict.h headfile.

(b) Now, suppose you want to restrict the dictionary data structure to be accessible only from functions in dict.c. You remove the declaration from dict.h. Is it still possible to directly access or modify the variable from main.c, even without the declaration in dict.h? If so, how would you ensure the data structure variable remains private?
ans:
using extern key word.

(c) Congratulations! You’re done and ready to compile your code. Write the command line that you should use to compile this code (using gcc). Let’s call the desired output program dictionary.o.
ans:
gcc -o0 -Wall main.c dict.c -o dictionary.o

Problem 3.3

Both the for loop and the do-while loop can be transformed into a simple while loop. For each of the following examples, write equivalent code using a while loop instead.
(a)

ans:

(b)

Note: you only need to modify the sample_geometric_rv() function.

ans:

Problem 3.4

’wc’ is a unix utility that display the count of characters, words and lines present in a file. If no file is specified it reads from the standard input. If more than one file name is specified it displays the counts for each file along with the filename. In this problem, we will be implementing wc.
One of the ways to build a complex program is to develop it iteratively, solving one problem at a time and testing it throroughly. For this problem, start with the following shell and then iteratively add the missing components.
ans:

Problem 3.5

In this problem, we will be reading in formatted data and generating a report. One of the common formats for interchange of formatted data is ’tab delimited’ where each line corresponds to a single record. The individual fields of the record are separated by tabs. For this problem, download the file stateoutflow0708.txt from Stellar. This contains the emigration of people from individual states. The first row of the file contains the column headings. There are eight self explanatory fields. Your task is to read the file using fscanf and generate a report outlining the migration of people from Massachusetts to all the other states. Use the field ”Aggr AGI” to report the numbers. Also, at the end, display a total and verify it is consistent with the one shown below. An example report should look like the following:

ans:
